3.2 Pressure in fluids at rest>Pascal's principle

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: What is the specific gravity of a substance that has a density of \(1350\,\text{kg/m}^3\)?
questions.hint: Specific gravity = density of substance / density of water (1000 kg/m³).
questions.explanation: Specific gravity is the ratio of the substance's density to that of water (\(1000\,\text{kg/m}^3\)). Thus \(SG = 1350 / 1000 = 1.35\). Option A is the reciprocal; Option C is the density itself, not the ratio.

explanations_tiered.definition: Specific gravity (relative density) is the ratio of a substance's density to the density of a reference substance, usually water at 4°C.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Since water has density \(1000\,\text{kg/m}^3\), the specific gravity is numerically equal to the density in g/cm³ and is dimensionless.
explanations_tiered.beginner: If a material is 1.35 times denser than water, it sinks. Specific gravity tells you how heavy something is compared to the same volume of water.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: \(SG = \rho / \rho_{\text{water}} = 1350 / 1000 = 1.35\). A value >1 means the substance sinks in water; <1 means it floats.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Specific gravity is dimensionless: \(SG = \frac{\rho}{\rho_{\text{water}}} = \frac{1350\,\text{kg/m}^3}{1000\,\text{kg/m}^3} = 1.35\). At 4°C, water density is maximum, making SG a precise reference.
reasoning_steps: Identify the formula for specific gravity.
Divide the substance's density by 1000 kg/m³.
misconceptions: Specific gravity has units (it does not; it is a ratio).
Confusing specific gravity with density (SG = 1.35 does not mean density is 1.35 kg/m³).

glossary_entry.definition: The ratio of the density of a substance to the density of water.

flashcard.front: Specific gravity of a 1350 kg/m³ substance?

publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Ratio of a substance's density to water's density.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Note that SG is the same as density in g/cm³.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A gas occupies \(2.0\,\text{m}^3\) at \(300\,\text{K}\) and \(150\,\text{kPa}\). If the temperature increases to \(450\,\text{K}\) at constant pressure, what is the new volume?
questions.hint: Use Charles's law: \(V_1/T_1 = V_2/T_2\) at constant pressure.
questions.explanation: For an ideal gas at constant pressure, \(V/T = \text{constant}\). Thus \(V_2 = V_1 \times (T_2/T_1) = 2.0 \times (450/300) = 2.0 \times 1.5 = 3.0\,\text{m}^3\). Option A inverts the ratio; Option D multiplies by T₂/T₁ incorrectly.

source_trace.content_anchor: PV = nRT, at constant P: V/T constant

explanations_tiered.definition: Ideal gas law: \(PV = nRT\). At constant pressure, volume is directly proportional to absolute temperature (Charles's law).
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: When pressure and amount of gas are fixed, increasing temperature increases the volume proportionally: \(V_2 = V_1 (T_2/T_1)\).
explanations_tiered.beginner: Heat makes gas particles move faster and push harder, so the volume expands if the container can stretch. Doubling the temperature (in kelvin) doubles the volume.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Using \(V_1/T_1 = V_2/T_2\): \(V_2 = V_1 \times T_2/T_1 = 2.0 \times 450/300 = 3.0\,\text{m}^3\). Temperature must be in kelvin.
explanations_tiered.advanced: From \(PV = nRT\), at constant \(P\) and \(n\), \(V/T = nR/P = \text{constant}\). Hence \(V_2 = V_1 (T_2/T_1)\). The calculation yields \(3.0\,\text{m}^3\).
worked_solution: Step 1: Identify constant pressure process → Charles's law: \(V_1/T_1 = V_2/T_2\).
misconceptions: Using Celsius instead of kelvin gives wrong ratio (450/300 vs 177/27).
Inverting temperature ratio (300/450) gives 1.33 m³, a common mistake.

glossary_entry.definition: Equation of state relating pressure, volume, temperature, and amount of an ideal gas.

flashcard.front: Gas at 2.0 m³, 300 K, constant P; new V at 450 K?

visual_system.visual_description: Linear graph: volume (m³) on y-axis, temperature (K) on x-axis; line through (300,2.0) and (450,3.0).
visual_system.caption: At constant pressure, volume is proportional to absolute temperature.
visual_system.accessibility_alt_text: Line graph: points at 300 K, 2.0 m³ and 450 K, 3.0 m³; straight line through origin.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Gas: 2.0 m³ at 300 K, constant P, new V at 450 K?
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: PV = nRT relates pressure, volume, temperature, and moles.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Solve problems related to pressure, density and specific gravity

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: An open manometer has a fluid column height difference of \(0.25\,\text{m}\) with the gas side lower. The fluid density is \(850\,\text{kg/m}^3\) and atmospheric pressure is \(101.3\,\text{kPa}\). What is the absolute gas pressure?
questions.hint: Gas side lower means \(P_{\text{gas}} = P_{\text{atm}} + \rho g h\).
questions.explanation: Since the gas side is lower, gas pressure exceeds atmospheric: \(P_{\text{gas}} = P_{\text{atm}} + \rho g h = 101.3\,\text{kPa} + (850 \times 9.8 \times 0.25)/1000 = 101.3 + 2.08 = 103.4\,\text{kPa}\). Option A subtracts; Option B ignores the column.
formula_used: \(P_{\text{gas}} = P_{\text{atm}} - \rho g h\) for gas side higher

explanations_tiered.definition: A manometer measures gas pressure relative to atmospheric pressure by balancing a fluid column.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: If the gas side is lower, \(P_{\text{gas}} = P_{\text{atm}} + \rho g h\); if higher, \(P_{\text{gas}} = P_{\text{atm}} - \rho g h\).
explanations_tiered.beginner: The fluid moves to the side with lower pressure. If gas pushes harder, the fluid rises higher on the open side.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Gas side lower means gas pressure > atmospheric. Calculate \(\rho g h = 850\times 9.8\times 0.25 = 2082\,\text{Pa} = 2.08\,\text{kPa}\). Then \(P_{\text{gas}} = 101.3 + 2.08 = 103.4\,\text{kPa}\).
explanations_tiered.advanced: Hydrostatic equilibrium: \(P_{\text{gas}} = P_{\text{atm}} + \rho g h\). The term \(\rho g h\) represents the gauge pressure. Option C is correct.
worked_solution: Step 1: Determine sign: gas side lower → add \(\rho g h\).
reasoning_steps: Interpret manometer reading (lower gas side = higher pressure).
misconceptions: Always adding \(\rho g h\) regardless of which side is higher; sign matters.
Forgetting to convert Pa to kPa or using incorrect units.

glossary_entry.definition: A U-tube device that measures gas pressure relative to atmospheric pressure.

flashcard.front: Open manometer: gas side lower, h=0.25 m, ρ=850 kg/m³, Patm=101.3 kPa. Find Pgas.

visual_system.visual_description: U-tube with fluid; left arm connected to gas, right arm open to air; gas side lower by height h.
visual_system.caption: Gas pressure equals atmospheric plus ρgh when gas side is lower.
visual_system.accessibility_alt_text: U-tube manometer: gas connection on left, open to air on right; left fluid level lower than right.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Manometer: gas side lower, h=0.25 m, ρ=850, Patm=101.3 kPa. Pgas?
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Device measuring gas pressure using a fluid column.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Draw the U-tube and show which side is higher.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Discuss the working principles of pressure measuring devices like manometers
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer C. ρgh = 2082 Pa = 2.08 kPa; Pgas = 101.3 + 2.08 = 103.4 kPa.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A metal block is completely submerged in water. The upward buoyant force is \(50\,\text{N}\). The block's weight in air is \(200\,\text{N}\). What is the block's apparent weight when submerged?
questions.hint: Apparent weight = weight in air − buoyant force.
questions.explanation: Apparent weight is the net downward force: \(W_{\text{app}} = W_{\text{air}} - F_B = 200\,\text{N} - 50\,\text{N} = 150\,\text{N}\). Option A equals the buoyant force; Option C ignores buoyancy; Option D adds instead of subtracts.

explanations_tiered.definition: Apparent weight is the net force measured by a scale when an object is submerged, equal to true weight minus buoyant force.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: When submerged, the buoyant force opposes gravity, reducing the net downward force. Thus the scale reads less than the actual weight.
explanations_tiered.beginner: If you try to lift a heavy rock underwater, it feels lighter. The water pushes up, helping you, so your arms feel less weight.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: The apparent weight = actual weight − buoyant force = \(200\,\text{N} - 50\,\text{N} = 150\,\text{N}\). The scale would read 150 N.
explanations_tiered.advanced: From Newton's second law: \(W_{\text{air}} - F_B - W_{\text{app}} = 0\) at equilibrium. Hence \(W_{\text{app}} = W_{\text{air}} - F_B\).
worked_solution: Step 1: Identify formula: apparent weight = weight in air − buoyant force.
reasoning_steps: Recall that buoyancy reduces the effective weight.
Subtract buoyant force from true weight.
misconceptions: Adding buoyant force to weight (thinking it pushes down).
Confusing apparent weight with buoyant force itself.

glossary_entry.definition: The weight of an object when submerged in a fluid, reduced by the buoyant force.

flashcard.front: True weight 200 N, buoyant force 50 N. Apparent weight?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: 200 N in air, 50 N buoyant. Apparent weight?
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Weight measured in a fluid, reduced by buoyancy.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Use a spring scale in water demo.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Solve problems related to buoyant force

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Water flows through a pipe of cross-sectional area \(0.05\,\text{m}^2\) at a speed of \(2.0\,\text{m/s}\). What is the volume flow rate?
questions.explanation: Flow rate is \(Q = A v = 0.05\,\text{m}^2 \times 2.0\,\text{m/s} = 0.10\,\text{m}^3/\text{s}\). Option A uses \(A/v\); Option C uses \(v/A\); Option D forgets to convert units.

explanations_tiered.definition: Volume flow rate is the volume of fluid that passes a cross-section per unit time: \(Q = Av\).
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Flow rate is the product of cross-sectional area and average flow speed. Units: m³/s (cubic meters per second).
explanations_tiered.beginner: Imagine a garden hose: the wider the hose (bigger A) or the faster water moves (bigger v), the more water comes out each second.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: \(Q = A v = 0.05\,\text{m}^2 \times 2.0\,\text{m/s} = 0.10\,\text{m}^3/\text{s}\). This means 0.10 cubic meters pass every second.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Flow rate is a scalar; for incompressible flow, \(Q\) is constant along a pipe (continuity). Units: \(1\,\text{m}^3/\text{s} = 1000\,\text{L/s}\).
reasoning_steps: Recall the definition of volume flow rate.
misconceptions: Using diameter instead of area in \(Q = Av\).
Confusing flow rate with velocity (they are different).

glossary_entry.definition: Volume of fluid crossing a cross-section per unit time.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Q for A=0.05 m², v=2 m/s?
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Compare with blood flow rate in aorta.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Water flows through a pipe that narrows from \(0.10\,\text{m}^2\) to \(0.02\,\text{m}^2\). If the speed in the wider section is \(1.5\,\text{m/s}\), what is the speed in the narrower section?
questions.explanation: For incompressible flow, \(A_1v_1 = A_2v_2\) → \(v_2 = v_1 \times (A_1/A_2) = 1.5 \times (0.10/0.02) = 1.5 \times 5 = 7.5\,\text{m/s}\). Option A uses \(A_2/A_1\); Option B uses a factor of 2 instead of 5.

explanations_tiered.definition: The equation of continuity states that for an incompressible fluid, the flow rate is constant: \(A_1 v_1 = A_2 v_2\).
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: When a pipe narrows, speed increases to maintain the same flow rate. The speed ratio is inversely proportional to area ratio.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Put your thumb over a garden hose: the opening gets smaller (area decreases), so water shoots out faster. Same idea.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Continuity derives from conservation of mass for incompressible flow: \(\nabla \cdot \vec{v} = 0\). Integrated: \(Q = A v = constant\).
worked_solution: Step 1: Write continuity: \(A_1 v_1 = A_2 v_2\).
reasoning_steps: Recall that flow rate is constant.
misconceptions: Using \(A_1 v_1 = A_2 v_2\) backwards (larger area gives larger speed).
Forgetting that fluids are incompressible for this form.

glossary_entry.definition: For incompressible flow, flow rate is constant: \(A_1 v_1 = A_2 v_2\).

flashcard.front: A₁=0.10 m², v₁=1.5 m/s; A₂=0.02 m². v₂?

visual_system.visual_description: Pipe wide on left (A₁=0.10 m², v₁=1.5 m/s), narrow on right (A₂=0.02 m², v₂=7.5 m/s).
visual_system.caption: Speed increases as area decreases to keep flow rate constant.
visual_system.accessibility_alt_text: Pipe diagram: wider left section with slower speed, narrower right section with faster speed.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Pipe narrows: A₁=0.10 m², v₁=1.5 m/s; A₂=0.02 m². v₂?
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Relate to blood flow in arteries vs capillaries.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Derive equation of continuity from flow rate

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subsection: Properties of solids, liquids and gases
questions.structural_anchor: Properties of solids, liquids and gases
questions.question: Why does a liquid take the shape of its container but maintain a nearly constant volume?

options.A: Atoms are fixed in position and cannot move
options.B: Intermolecular forces are negligible, allowing free expansion
options.C: Atoms are close together but can slide past each other, held by mutual attraction
options.D: The atoms are far apart and collide elastically

questions.hint: Think about particle spacing and mobility in liquids.
questions.explanation: Liquids have atoms in close contact (constant volume) but can slide and change neighbors (take container shape), with intermolecular forces keeping them together. Option A describes solids; Option B and D describe gases.

source_trace.subsection_anchor: Properties of solids, liquids and gases
source_trace.structural_element: Properties of solids, liquids and gases
source_trace.content_anchor: Liquids deform easily when stressed … molecules held together by mutual attraction

explanations_tiered.definition: A liquid is a fluid with particles in close contact but able to slide past one another, allowing it to flow while maintaining constant volume.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Liquids have intermediate particle spacing: close enough to resist compression (constant volume), but not fixed in position (shape adapts).
explanations_tiered.beginner: Liquid is like a box of marbles: they touch but can roll around, so the pile takes the shape of whatever you pour it into, but the amount of marbles stays the same.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Intermolecular forces in liquids are strong enough to keep particles together (fixed volume) but not strong enough to lock positions, allowing flow and shape change.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Liquid molecules have sufficient kinetic energy to overcome some intermolecular bonds but remain within the potential well, giving fluidity with incompressibility.
misconceptions: Liquids are compressible (they are nearly incompressible).
Liquids have no intermolecular forces (they do, and these maintain constant volume).
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Liquid: close particles, slide, constant volume.

glossary_entry.definition: A state of matter with definite volume but indefinite shape, particles close but mobile.

flashcard.front: Why does liquid take container shape but keep volume?
flashcard.back: Particles are close (constant volume) but can slide past each other (take shape).

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Property: liquid constant volume but variable shape?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Close particles that can slide; forces keep volume but allow flow.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Matter with fixed volume but no fixed shape.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Compare with solid (fixed shape) and gas (no fixed volume).
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Explain the properties of solids, liquids and gases
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer C. Close particles (constant volume) but can slide (adapt shape).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A person standing on one foot exerts more pressure on the ground than when standing on two feet because pressure depends on:

options.A: Only the person's mass, not area

questions.explanation: Pressure is force per area. Standing on one foot halves the contact area compared to two feet, so pressure doubles (same force). Option A ignores area; Option C multiplies instead of divides.

source_trace.content_anchor: P = F/A, smaller area gives larger pressure

explanations_tiered.definition: Pressure is force per unit area; for the same force, smaller area produces greater pressure.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Reducing contact area concentrates the force, increasing pressure. This is why sharp objects cut and high heels sink into soft ground.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Step on a scale with one foot: the number is the same (weight), but your foot feels like it pushes harder because all your weight is on a smaller spot.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Pressure \(P = F/A\). Weight \(F\) is constant. One foot has half the area of two feet, so pressure doubles: \(P_1 = F/(A/2) = 2F/A\).
explanations_tiered.advanced: From \(P = F/A\), pressure inversely proportional to area at constant force. One-foot standing increases pressure by factor \(A_{\text{two}}/A_{\text{one}} = 2\).
misconceptions: Thinking pressure depends on area only (it depends on both force and area).
Believing pressure is force (pressure is force per area).

flashcard.front: Why more pressure on one foot than two?
flashcard.back: Same force, smaller contact area → larger pressure (P = F/A).

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: One foot vs two feet: why more pressure?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Same weight, smaller area → higher pressure (P = F/A).
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Demonstrate with a block on foam.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Pressure = force ÷ area; smaller area gives larger pressure.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: What causes the pressure exerted by a gas on the walls of its container?

options.A: Strong intermolecular forces pulling the walls inward
options.B: Gas particles colliding elastically with the walls
options.C: The gas particles sticking to the walls permanently
options.D: The weight of the gas compressing the bottom

questions.hint: Think about kinetic theory of gases.
questions.explanation: Gas pressure arises from countless particles colliding with the container walls, each collision exerting an impulse. Option A is more relevant to liquids; Option C is incorrect; Option D is only part of gravitational effect but not the primary cause.

source_trace.content_anchor: particle exerts an impulsive force on the object … collective collisions exert force

explanations_tiered.definition: Gas pressure is the result of gas molecules colliding with the container walls, transferring momentum and exerting force.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Each collision exerts a tiny force; with billions of collisions per second, the net effect is a steady pressure proportional to particle density and temperature.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Imagine tiny balls bouncing off the walls of a box. Each ball gives a little push. With many balls bouncing very fast, they push the walls outward constantly.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: From kinetic theory, \(P = \frac{1}{3} \frac{N}{V} m v_{\text{rms}}^2\). Pressure arises from momentum transfer during elastic collisions with the walls.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Pressure \(P = \frac{F}{A} = \frac{\Delta p/\Delta t}{A}\). For an ideal gas, \(P = \frac{2}{3} \frac{N}{V} \langle E_k \rangle\). The collision rate and impulse determine macroscopic pressure.
reasoning_steps: Recall that gas particles are in constant random motion.
Each collision exerts force; net force per area = pressure.
misconceptions: Gravity causes gas pressure (only a small part; most comes from collisions).
Gas pressure is due to repulsive forces (no, it's collisions).

glossary_entry.definition: Pressure exerted by gas particles colliding with container walls.

flashcard.front: What causes gas pressure in a container?
flashcard.back: Gas particles colliding elastically with the walls.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Collisions of gas particles with container walls.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Pressure from molecular collisions with walls.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Use a simulation of particles in a box.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Explain causes of pressure in gases
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Gas pressure is due to particles colliding with container walls.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Two objects have the same volume. Object A has density \(500\,\text{kg/m}^3\), object B has density \(1500\,\text{kg/m}^3\). Which statement is correct?

options.A: A has greater mass than B
options.B: B has greater mass than A
options.D: Mass depends on shape, not density

questions.explanation: Mass \(m = \rho V\). With equal volume, higher density means higher mass. Object B has triple the density of A, so B has three times the mass. Option A is opposite; Option C ignores density difference.

explanations_tiered.definition: Density is mass per unit volume; for a fixed volume, mass is proportional to density.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: If two objects have identical volume, the denser object contains more matter and therefore has greater mass.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Imagine two same-sized boxes: one filled with cotton (low density) and one filled with lead (high density). The lead box is much heavier.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: From \(m = \rho V\), with \(V\) constant, \(m \propto \rho\). Object B has \(\rho_B = 1500 > \rho_A = 500\), so \(m_B > m_A\).
explanations_tiered.advanced: Mass is an extensive property; density is intensive. For equal volume, mass ratio equals density ratio: \(m_B/m_A = 1500/500 = 3\).
reasoning_steps: Compare densities; higher density → higher mass at equal volume.
misconceptions: Thinking density and mass are the same thing.
Assuming larger density always means larger object (volume matters too).
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Same V: higher ρ → higher m.

flashcard.front: Equal V: ρA=500, ρB=1500. Which has greater mass?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Equal V: ρ=500 vs ρ=1500; which heavier?
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Use a balance and equal-volume cylinders of different materials.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. m = ρV, same V → larger ρ gives larger mass.
